evidence: Docstring asks for a composite number m with n + f(n) < m < n + p(m), where p(m) is the least prime factor of m.

The set A consists of n expressible as a sum of distinct proper divisors (pseudoperfect/semiperfect numbers) minimal under divisibility, and the problem asks whether the sum of their reciprocals converges.

The statement is Bloom's [Bl21] 'density conjecture about unit fractions' — every A with harmonic sum > delta log N contains S with sum of reciprocals 1 — the strong density form resolving the Erdős–Graham problem.
The statement is about subsets whose unit fractions (reciprocals) sum to 1; references [Bl21] and [LiSa24] are titled on unit fractions and erdosproblems.com tags it 'unit fractions'.
Docstring asks 'Are there any odd weird numbers?', the statement quantifying over n.Weird.
Docstring asks 'Are there infinitely many primitive weird numbers?', primitive weird being weird numbers with no weird proper divisor.
Docstring: Melfi's result is conditional on p_{n+1} - p_n < sqrt(p_n)/10, 'well-known conjectures concerning prime gaps'.
Docstring: 'This is the Erdős-Heilbronn inequality, proved by Dias da Silva and Hamidoune' — |A+^A| >= min(2|A|-3, p) for A in F_p.
The statement bounds the cardinality of A.restrictedSumset, the restricted sumset A+^A = {a+b : a != b in A}.
The statement asks whether for all k > 1 there are infinitely many n with 2^n = k (mod n), a congruence condition on n.
Docstring asks whether there are infinitely many n, m with phi(n) = sigma(m), phi being Euler's totient (n.totient in the statement).
The statement equates phi(n) with the sum-of-divisors function sigma(m) (σ 1 m via ArithmeticFunction.sigma).
Chung and Graham's constant c = 1 + sum 1/F_{2k} 'where F_m is the mth Fibonacci number' per the docstring.
The best-possible statement uses the same Fibonacci-defined constant c = 1 + sum 1/F_{2k}.
